meta.title: ForgeZero Documentation
meta.description: Zero-overhead build tool for assembly, C, and Gloria

blocks.content: ForgeZero is a high-performance, zero-overhead build tool for assembly, C, C++, Objective-C, and Gloria developers. It wraps NASM, GAS, FASM, GCC, Clang, Zig, and LD into a single unified command-line interface — no Makefiles, no build scripts, no configuration required to get started.
Inspired by the simplicity of Suckless and the efficiency of TinyCC
Non nobis, Domine, non nobis, sed nomini tuo da Gloriam

Benchmarks measured against standard nasm -f elf64 && ld and make -j4 pipelines. Test environment: Intel Core i5-10310U (4C/8T, 1.7 GHz base), Arch Linux, Samsung 980 NVMe. Results are mean ± stddev over ≥10 runs via hyperfine.

Conclusion: ForgeZero maintains ~3–5× speedup at scale, growing to nearly 5× at 400 modules. Traditional pipelines suffer from super-linear overhead due to process spawning, I/O contention, and CPU cache thrashing. ForgeZero's single-process design preserves cache locality across the entire build.

rows: Writes N intermediate .o files to disk
Zero-allocation hot path (0 allocs/op, 0 B/op)

blocks.content: Note: Projections beyond 400 modules assume continued sub-linear growth. Real-world results vary based on I/O and CPU contention.

# Clone and build ForgeZero
git clone https://github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero
cd ForgeZero
go build -o fz ./cmd/fz

# Run the benchmark script (generates N test modules and runs hyperfine)
./bench.sh  # Edit NUM_MODULES in script for different module counts

# Export benchmark results to Markdown
hyperfine --warmup 3 --prepare 'make clean && rm -rf .fz_objs fz_out' \
  './fz -dir . -out fz_out' 'make -j4' \
  --export-markdown results.md

.asm                nasm              x86/x86-64 Intel syntax
  .s  / .S            gcc (drives as)   AT&T syntax; .S is C-preprocessed
  .fasm               fasm              Not bundled; get from
                                         flatassembler.net
  .c                  gcc or clang      Strict flags + sanitizers by default
  .cpp .cc .cxx       g++ or clang++    Same strict flags as C
  .m                  clang or zig cc   Objective-C; auto framework linking
  .glo                built-in (HADES)  No external tool required

gcc     Default linking, C runtime support
  ld      Raw linking (-mode raw), linker scripts, direct -ld invocation
  clang   Strict sanitizer mode (-strict), Objective-C compilation
  ar      Static library mode (-type static)

When using -target <triple>, fz looks for prefixed toolchain binaries
  on PATH:

arm-linux-gnueabihf    arm-linux-gnueabihf-gcc
aarch64-linux-gnu      aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc
riscv64-linux-gnu      riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc
riscv64-linux-musl     riscv64-linux-musl-gcc, or -zig
x86_64-linux-gnu       x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc

When using -zig, no prefixed toolchain is required; Zig resolves the
  target internally.

nm          Pre-link duplicate symbol check, primary
  objdump     Fallback for symbol check
  readelf     Second fallback for symbol check
  git         Required for fz pm add
  zig         Required for -zig backend
  emcc        Required for wasm32-emscripten target
  hyperfine   Benchmarking (fz bench and bench.sh), optional
Go 1.21 or later is required only to build fz from source.

sudo apt update
  sudo apt install -y nasm gcc binutils git
  sudo apt install -y clang
  sudo apt install -y gcc-arm-linux-gnueabihf
  sudo apt install -y gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu
  sudo apt install -y gcc-riscv64-linux-gnu

Zig, optional: wget https://ziglang.org/download/0.13.0/zig-linux-x86_64-0.13.0.tar.xz tar -xf zig-linux-x86_64-0.13.0.tar.xz sudo mv zig-linux-x86_64-0.13.0 /opt/zig echo export PATH="$PATH:/opt/zig" >> ~/.bashrc
FASM, optional: wget https://flatassembler.net/fasm-1.73.32.tgz tar -xzf fasm-1.73.32.tgz sudo cp fasm/fasm /usr/local/bin/ chmod +x /usr/local/bin/fasm

Install fz:
    go install github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero/cmd/fz@latest
    echo export PATH="$PATH:$(go env GOPATH)/bin" >> ~/.bashrc
    fz -v

Fedora:
    sudo dnf install -y nasm gcc binutils clang git

RHEL / CentOS, nasm requires EPEL:
    sudo dnf install -y epel-release
    sudo dnf install -y nasm gcc binutils clang git

sudo pacman -S --noconfirm nasm gcc binutils clang git zig
  yay -S fasm     (AUR)
  go install github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero/cmd/fz@latest

sudo zypper install -y nasm gcc binutils clang git
  go install github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero/cmd/fz@latest

Support is in progress per source. The following setup covers most
  current use cases.

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
brew install nasm gcc go git zig
go install github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero/cmd/fz@latest
echo export PATH="$PATH:$(go env GOPATH)/bin" >> ~/.zshrc
fz -v

Note: the Darwin syscall layer is stubbed. Builds succeed; runtime
  behavior is stated as untested. Objective-C on macOS requires Xcode
  Command Line Tools (xcode-select --install).

Option A, WSL2, recommended:
    wsl --install
    (restart, open Ubuntu, follow the Debian/Ubuntu section above)

Option B, native Windows, experimental, via MSYS2:
    1. Install MSYS2 from msys2.org. In the MSYS2 MinGW 64-bit shell:
         pacman -Syu
         pacman -S mingw-w64-x86_64-gcc mingw-w64-x86_64-binutils
         pacman -S mingw-w64-x86_64-clang git
    2. Install NASM for Windows from nasm.us.
    3. Add to PATH: msys64 mingw64 bin, the NASM install directory,
       and the go bin directory.
    4. Install Go from go.dev/dl.
    5. go install github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero/cmd/fz@latest

Known limitation: -sanitize and -strict require a Windows Clang
  build with AddressSanitizer support; not covered by plain MSYS2.

Run fz doctor immediately after install to confirm PATH and
  directory permissions.
git clone https://github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero.git cd ForgeZero go build -o fz ./cmd/fz/main.go (Linux / macOS) go build -o fz.exe ./cmd/fz/main.go (Windows)

sudo mv fz /usr/local/bin/                         (Linux / macOS)
  Move-Item fz.exe C:\Windows\System32\fz.exe         (Windows, Admin)

fz -v         short version string
  fz --version  full platform / build metadata block

At least one of -asm, -cc, -gloria, -dir, -init, -shell, -contribute,
  pm, sbom, audit, verify, bench, doctor, or a valid config file must
  be present.

-asm <file>        --            Assemble the given assembly source
                                    file.
  -cc <file>         --            Compile the given C, C++, or
                                    Objective-C source file.
  -gloria <file>     --            Compile the given Gloria (.glo)
                                    source file via the built-in HADES
                                    engine.
  -dir <dir>         --            Recursively build all supported
                                    files in the directory.
  -out <name>        derived       Name of the output binary.
  -out-obj <name>    <base>.o      Object file name, single-file mode
                                    only.
  -profile, -p       balanced      Apply a named build profile:
                                    performance, balanced, power-saver.
                                    Persisted to
                                    ~/.config/fz/.profile.config.
  -mode              auto          Linking mode: auto, c, or raw. See
                                    LINKING MODES below.
  -ld                off           Invoke the linker directly,
                                    bypassing compiler validation
                                    layers. Reduces overhead about
                                    3 to 5 percent on small projects.
  -format             elf64        Output format for assembled
                                    binaries: elf32, elf64, or bin.
  -target <triple>   --            Cross-compilation target triple,
                                    e.g. arm-linux-gnueabihf,
                                    wasm32-wasi.
  -zig               off           Use Zig (zig cc / zig c++) as the
                                    compiler backend.
  --reproducible     off           Enable deterministic builds:
                                    suppress build IDs, timestamps,
                                    path references; sort objects.
  --arena-size=N     auto          Override default arena capacity
                                    for plugin execution. Advanced
                                    use only.
  -type              executable    Output type: linked binary or
                                    static library (.a).
  -lib <name>        --            Output library name when -type
                                    static is used, without the lib
                                    prefix or .a suffix.
  -shared            off           Build a shared library
                                    (.so / .dylib / .dll).
  -cc-flag <flags>   --            Extra compiler flags, space
                                    separated, injected after
                                    standard flags.
  -ld-flag <flags>   --            Extra linker flags, space
                                    separated, appended to the linker
                                    command.
  -j <N>             profile-dep   Parallel compilation jobs. 0 means
                                    auto, number of CPU cores.
                                    Overridden by -profile.
  -T <script>        --            Linker script passed to ld.
  -Ttext <addr>      --            Entry point address passed to the
                                    linker, hex or decimal.
  -debug             off           Pass -g to the assembler/compiler
                                    to emit debug symbols.
  -verbose           off           Print each external command to
                                    stdout before running it.
  -keep-obj          off           Preserve object files after
                                    linking, directory mode.
  -no-cache          off           Disable the build cache; always
                                    recompile every file.
  -no-symbol-check   off           Skip the pre-link duplicate symbol
                                    check.
  -sanitize          on            Enable -fsanitize=address,undefined
                                    for C/C++. Disable with
                                    -sanitize=false.
  -strict            off           Stricter sanitizers plus
                                    use-after-return/scope checks.
                                    Prefers clang/clang++.
  -json              off           Suppress normal output; emit a
                                    JSON build report to stdout.
  -watch             off           Watch source files for changes and
                                    rebuild automatically.
  -clean             off           Remove all build artifacts and
                                    exit.
  -compile-commands  off           Generate compile_commands.json for
                                    LSP/IDE integration.
  -init              off           Scaffold a new project: creates
                                    .fz.yaml, .fzignore, README.md.
  -shell             off           Open interactive REPL shell.
  -update            off           Download and install the latest fz
                                    binary; backs up current binary to
                                    fz.old.
  -config <file>     auto-detect   Path to a YAML configuration file.
  -timeout <sec>     60            Timeout in seconds for each
                                    sub-command.
  -manifest <file>   .fz.manifest  Path to the BLAKE3 source manifest
                                    used by fz verify.
  -h, --help         --            Print help and exit.
  -v                 --            Print short version string and
                                    exit, e.g. 4.7.0.
  --version          --            Print full version details:
                                    platform, Go version, build
                                    metadata.

fz pm add <repo>[@version]   Clone a package from a Git repository
                                and register it in .fz.yaml.
  fz pm remove <package>       Remove a package; clean up .fz.yaml
                                and empty parent directories.
  fz pm list                   List all installed packages.
  fz pm update                 Update all installed packages to the
                                latest commit or tag.
  fz pm catalog                Browse the official ForgeZero package
                                catalog.
  fz pm search <query>         Search the catalog by name or keyword.
  fz pm install <name>         Install a package from the catalog
                                with BLAKE3 hash verification.

fz sbom                      Generate a CycloneDX SBOM with BLAKE3
                                hashes for all build components.
  fz sbom -o <path>            Write the SBOM to a specific output
                                file, default sbom.cdx.json.
  fz sbom -dir <dir>           Generate SBOM scoped to a specific
                                source directory.
  fz audit                     Run the built-in SAST scanner: secrets,
                                license compliance, dangerous
                                patterns.
  fz audit -dir <dir>          Audit a specific directory.
  fz audit -json               Emit the audit report as JSON.
  fz verify                    Verify the current source tree against
                                the stored BLAKE3 manifest.
  fz verify --generate         Generate a new BLAKE3 manifest of the
                                current source tree.
  fz verify --strict           Also report UNTRACKED files not in the
                                manifest.
  fz verify -manifest <f>      Use a specific manifest file instead of
                                the default .fz.manifest.
  fz doctor                    Run the Aegis self-audit: toolchain,
                                permissions, platform integrity.
  fz doctor -root <dir>        Audit a specific project root, default
                                current working directory.
  fz doctor -json              Emit the audit report as JSON; exit
                                code 1 if healthy is false.

fz bench                     Profile the build: nanosecond
                                precision timing per phase.
  fz bench -n <N>               Run N times; report average and
                                standard deviation per phase.
  fz bench -json                Emit the benchmark report as JSON.

fz contribute                 Generate CONTRIBUTING_USER.md with
                                environment diagnostics and
                                contributor guidance.

Named presets configuring CPU core usage, parallel job count, and
  compiler optimization level in a single flag. Persisted between
  runs in ~/.config/fz/.profile.config and applied automatically on
  subsequent invocations unless overridden.

performance    all cores    -O3    all cores    max build speed,
                                                 CI, release
balanced       half cores   -O2    half cores    default, daily
                                                  development
power-saver    1 core       -Os    1 core        battery
                                                  constrained

Usage:
    fz -p performance -cc main.c
    fz -p power-saver -dir ./src
    fz -profile balanced -dir ./src

Reset to default:
    rm ~/.config/fz/.profile.config
    fz -profile balanced -cc main.c

Flags that directly override profile-managed settings take
  precedence over the profile, e.g. fz -p performance -dir ./src -j 2
  uses -j 2 even though performance would otherwise set all cores.

GOMAXPROCS is set internally by fz for the duration of the build; it
  does not affect the calling shell environment.

In a config file:
    profile: performance
  CLI -profile / -p takes precedence over the config file value.

auto (default)
    fz tries linkers in sequence until one succeeds: gcc (standard
    linking with libc and C runtime), gcc -no-pie (position-dependent
    executable, for code assuming fixed load addresses), ld (raw
    system linker, last resort). When -strict is active, clang with
    full sanitizer flags is tried first.

c, force gcc / clang
    fz -asm program.asm -mode c
    fz -cc main.c -mode c
    Always links using gcc, or clang in strict mode. Required when
    code calls libc functions (printf, malloc, exit) or depends on C
    runtime initialization.

raw, force ld
    fz -asm kernel.asm -mode raw -out kernel.bin
    Bypasses gcc entirely and invokes ld directly. Suitable for OS
    kernels, bootloaders, bare-metal firmware, programs defining their
    own _start and using raw syscalls, and embedded targets requiring
    full control over binary layout. Raw-linked binaries cannot
    reference any libc symbol.

direct linker invocation, -ld
    fz -asm boot.asm -ld -out boot.elf
    Invokes the linker directly, bypassing compiler validation layers
    entirely. Reduces orchestration overhead about 3 to 5 percent on
    small projects. Unlike -mode raw, -ld does not imply a particular
    linking strategy; it removes the gcc/clang wrapper from the
    invocation chain.

0   Success. Binary produced; or fz verify / fz audit found no
      violations; or fz doctor is healthy.
  1   Build error. Assembler, compiler, or linker failed; duplicate
      global symbol; fz verify found MODIFIED or MISSING files; fz
      audit found WARNING or above; fz doctor is degraded.
  2   Argument error. Invalid or missing flags, source file not
      found, cross-compiler not on PATH, unreadable config file.

fz wraps NASM, GAS, FASM, GCC, Clang, Zig, and LD into a single
  command-line interface. Given a source file or directory, fz:

- detects file type and selects the correct assembler backend
- compiles each source file to an object file with appropriate
  flags
- checks for duplicate global symbols across all objects before
  linking
- links everything into a single binary using the most
  appropriate linker
- caches compiled objects using BLAKE3 so unchanged files are
  never recompiled

Per source, the traditional pipeline (nasm/gcc + ld, driven by make)
  spawns one process per compiled unit plus the linker, writes an
  intermediate object file to disk per unit, and starts each fork with
  a cold CPU cache. fz is described as a single integrated process
  using goroutines for parallelism instead of OS-level process
  forking, holding intermediate state in memory instead of writing it
  to disk, and preserving cache locality across the whole build as a
  result.

fz is a single statically compiled Go binary with no runtime
  dependencies beyond the assembler/compiler toolchain actually
  invoked for a given source type.

Measured against nasm -f elf64 && ld and make -j4 pipelines. Test
  environment: Intel Core i5-10310U, 4 cores / 8 threads, 1.7 GHz
  base, Arch Linux, Samsung 980 NVMe. Results are mean plus/minus
  standard deviation over 10 or more runs via hyperfine.

metric                          fz          make -j4
------------------------------  ----------  ----------
time growth, 20 to 400 modules  +1056%      +2333%
overhead per module             ~0.36 ms    ~1.23 ms
I/O operations                  0 interm.   2x modules
                                 files       (.o read/write)
process forks                   1           ~2x modules + 1

Stated conclusion: fz maintains roughly 3 to 5x speedup at scale,
  growing to nearly 5x at 400 modules; traditional pipelines suffer
  super-linear overhead from process spawning, I/O contention, and CPU
  cache thrashing.

Scaling projection (source-labeled as projection/estimate beyond
  400 modules, assuming continued sub-linear growth; real-world
  results stated to vary with I/O and CPU contention):

git clone https://github.com/forgezero-cli/ForgeZero
cd ForgeZero
go build -o fz ./cmd/fz
./bench.sh
hyperfine --warmup 3 --prepare "make clean && rm -rf .fz_objs
  fz_out" "./fz -dir . -out fz_out" "make -j4"
  --export-markdown results.md

Build cache, BLAKE3
    Compiled objects are cached in .fz_cache/. Cache key is computed
    from the BLAKE3 hash of source contents plus the -debug flag
    state, -mode value, -target value, and active build profile.
    cache_mode in project config selects disk (default, on-disk
    caching), ram (in-memory only, no disk writes), or off (disabled).
    -no-cache forces cache mode to off. BLAKE3 dropped 10 MB file
    hash time from about 58 ms, pre-2.0.0 SHA256, to about 8.7 ms.

Pre-link symbol check
    Before invoking the linker, fz scans all compiled objects for
    duplicate global symbol definitions, using nm first, then objdump,
    then readelf as fallbacks. A conflict is reported with the
    offending files and exits code 1. Disable with -no-symbol-check.

Watch mode
    fz -dir ./src -watch uses fsnotify for cross-platform filesystem
    events. Rebuilds are debounced with a 500 millisecond delay.

JSON output
    -json suppresses normal output and writes one JSON object to
    stdout on completion: status, exit_code, duration_ms, binary,
    source_files, object_files, error.

Clean
    fz -dir . -clean removes .fz_objs/, .fz_cache/, all .o files, and
    executable files identified by the +x permission bit.

Parallel builds
    -j N compiles up to N files simultaneously; -j 0 uses all
    available CPU cores. When a profile is active, -j is set
    automatically by the profile; an explicit -j always overrides it.
    The parallel build and logging pipeline is stated as
    race-condition-free, verified with go test -race.

Interactive shell
    fz -shell opens a REPL with commands: build <file>, build -dir
    <dir>, set <flag> <value>, clean, help, exit.

Virtual filesystem layer
    All durable and security-sensitive I/O routes through an
    internal/fs FileSystem interface rather than calling os.Open,
    os.WriteFile, or os.Rename directly. Full detail is in INTERNALS.

See LANGUAGES for per-source-type compilation behavior, WORKFLOW for
  configuration and supply-chain tooling, and INTERNALS for the
  virtual filesystem layer, the Aegis security core, and the HADES
  codegen/ELF engine.

.asm             Assembly       NASM              x86/x86-64 Intel
                                                     syntax, ELF64
  .s               Assembly       GAS via gcc -c    AT&T syntax
  .S               Assembly       GAS via gcc -c    AT&T syntax, C
                                                     preprocessed
  .fasm            Assembly       FASM              separate install;
                                                     auto format ELF64
                                                     injection
  .c               C              GCC, Clang, or    strict flags plus
                                   zig cc            sanitizers by
                                                     default
  .cpp .cc .cxx    C++            G++, Clang++, or  same strict flags
                                   zig c++           as C
  .m               Objective-C    Clang or zig cc   auto framework
                                                     linking
  .glo             Gloria         built-in, HADES   compiles to raw
                                                     x86-64 ELF,
                                                     69 to 125 bytes

All other extensions are silently ignored during directory and
  recursive scanning.

Every .c file receives these flags unconditionally:
    -Wall -Wextra -Werror -Wpedantic -Wshadow -Wconversion
Any warning is treated as an error and stops the build immediately.
Sanitizers are automatically disabled for wasm32-* targets.

fz compiles .cpp, .cc, and .cxx with g++ or clang++, or zig c++ when
  -zig is active. The same strict warning flags as C apply, and
  sanitizers are enabled by default the same way as C.

fz -cc main.cpp
fz -cc main.cc
fz -cc main.cxx
fz -dir ./src     (mixed C/C++ directory; dispatched per extension,
                   linked in one step)

.m files are compiled automatically via Clang by default, or Zig
  when -zig is active. Pipeline: source recognition, backend
  selection, compilation with -x objective-c and standard flags, then
  automatic linking of the Objective-C runtime (-lobjc) and any
  required system frameworks.

fz -cc main.m
fz -cc main.m -verbose
fz -dir ./src
fz -cc main.m -zig

Clang must be on PATH; on Linux this covers use cases not requiring
  macOS-specific frameworks (Foundation, AppKit). macOS-specific
  Objective-C frameworks are not available on Linux; code using only
  the GNU Objective-C runtime compiles on Linux without issue. Using
  -zig, Zig's bundled Clang frontend handles .m files with no separate
  Clang install needed.

Gloria is ForgeZero's integrated systems programming language,
  compiling directly to raw x86-64 ELF binaries via the HADES engine
  with no external assembler, compiler, or linker required. Designed
  for bare-metal targets, OS kernels, firmware.

Key properties: Go/Rust-like minimal syntax; compiled output 69 to
  125 bytes for typical programs; no runtime dependencies; bare-metal
  VGA framebuffer output via print() in kernel mode; x86-64 I/O port
  access via in8()/out8(); arbitrary memory read/write via
  peek()/poke().

Supported constructs: variable declaration (let x = 42), arithmetic
  (+, -=, *=), function definition/call (fn add(a, b), add(1, 2)),
  conditional (if x { ... }, executes if x is non-zero), loop
  (while x { ... }, iterates while x is non-zero, supports =, +=, -=
  and builtin calls in the body), print("hello").

Memory and I/O:
    peek(address)          reads a 16-bit value; address may be an
                            immediate or a variable
    poke(address, value)   writes a 16-bit value; both args may be
                            immediates or variables
    in8(port)               reads one byte from an x86-64 I/O port,
                            zero-extended to 64 bits
    out8(port, value)      writes one byte to an x86-64 I/O port

VGA framebuffer output: in bare-metal/kernel mode, print() writes
  directly to the VGA text-mode framebuffer at address 0xB8000 using
  green text, attribute byte 0x0A. Register R15 is reserved as the VGA
  cursor offset and preserved across function calls; escape sequences
  \n and \t are resolved at compile time. In userspace mode (default),
  print() uses the Linux sys_write syscall on file descriptor 1; the
  kernel/userspace dispatch is automatic based on compilation target.

Register constants: named constants exist for all 16 x86-64
  general-purpose registers (regRAX through regR15, encodings 0
  through 15) for codegen clarity; R15 must not be used as a
  general-purpose variable in kernel Gloria programs.

Compilation pipeline: lexer tokenizes .glo source, disambiguating
  CPU instruction mnemonics from user labels at the lexer/parser
  boundary; parser builds an AST, with malformed input triggering an
  explicit malformed-AST error before codegen, no silent fallback
  degradation; codegen (HADES) emits x86-64 machine code using
  zero-allocation stack buffers; ELF emission writes a valid ELF64
  binary with local-before-global symbol table ordering and
  deterministic relocation offsets. The entire pipeline runs in a
  single process with zero intermediate files on disk.

fz -gloria main.glo
fz -gloria main.glo -verbose
fz -gloria main.glo -out kernel.elf

FASM (.fasm)
    Command: fasm <file> <output.o>
    fz automatically injects "format ELF64 executable" when no format
    directive is found in the source, and passes -dDEBUG=1 when
    -debug is active.

The Zig compiler ships as a single self-contained binary including a
  full copy of musl libc, glibc stubs, and a WASI sysroot for every
  supported target, plus a Clang-based C/C++ frontend, making fz
  genuinely zero-dependency for cross-compilation when active.

fz -cc main.c -zig
fz -dir ./src -zig
fz -cc main.c -zig -target aarch64-linux-musl
fz -cc main.c -zig -target riscv64-linux-musl
fz -cc main.c -zig -target x86_64-windows-gnu

flags                    C compiler        C++ compiler
------------------------  ----------------  ----------------
default                   gcc or clang      g++ or clang++
-zig                      zig cc            zig c++
-zig -target T            zig cc -target T  zig c++ -target T
-zig -strict              zig cc, full san.  zig c++

fz -cc main.c -target arm-linux-gnueabihf
fz -cc main.c -target aarch64-linux-gnu
fz -cc main.c -target riscv64-linux-gnu
fz -cc main.c -zig -target aarch64-linux-musl
fz -cc main.c -zig -target riscv64-linux-musl

Without -zig, fz constructs prefixed compiler/linker/archiver names
  by prepending the triple: <triple>-gcc, <triple>-g++, <triple>-gcc
  or <triple>-ld depending on linking mode, <triple>-ar for static
  libraries. With -zig, the triple is passed directly to
  zig cc -target; no prefixed binary lookup happens, and Zig ships all
  libc variants internally, making it the recommended backend for
  static musl targets including riscv64-linux-musl.

wasm32-emscripten   Emscripten / browser   full libc emulation, JS
                                              glue loader
  wasm32-wasi         Wasmtime, WasmEdge,    server-side / cloud-native
                      WAMR                   modules

fz -cc main.c -zig -target wasm32-wasi -out main.wasm
wasmtime main.wasm

source /path/to/emsdk/emsdk_env.sh
fz -cc main.c -target wasm32-emscripten -out main.js

ASan/UBSan are automatically disabled for wasm32-* targets; pass
  -sanitize=false to suppress the notice.

fz searches the working directory for a config file in this order:
  .fz.yaml, fz.yaml, .fz.yml, fz.yml. Run fz with no flags to use it,
  or fz -config <path> to specify one explicitly. CLI flags always
  take precedence over config file values.

Config merging, three levels, in priority order:
    1. system level:   /etc/fz/fz.yaml
    2. user level:      ~/.config/fz/fz.yaml
    3. project level:   .fz.yaml in the working directory
    4. CLI flags        (highest priority)

Basic fields: source_dir, source_dirs, source_files, output, mode,
  format, target, backend, profile, reproducible, type, lib, jobs,
  debug, verbose, keep_obj, no_cache, cache_mode, sanitize, strict,
  ignore_file.

Multiple source directories are prefixed into object file names,
  e.g. kernel/boot.asm becomes kernel_boot_asm.o. source_files gives
  an exact file list and takes precedence over source_dirs and
  source_dir; each path is verified at startup.

Include/exclude evaluation order: exclude, then .fzignore, then the
  symlink boundary check, then include, then supported extensions.

Library linking:
    libs:
      - m          (-lm)
      - pthread    (-lpthread)
      - c          (-lc)

Custom flags:
    flags:
      asm: [ -DDEBUG_BUILD, -I./include ]
      cc:  [ -O3, -march=native, -DNDEBUG, -ffreestanding ]
      ld:  [ -T, linker.ld, -Map, output.map ]

.fzignore works exactly like .gitignore and is evaluated after
  exclude patterns.

Creates .fz.yaml (minimal config with commented fields), .fzignore
  (default ignore rules), README.md (project template with fz build
  instructions). No existing file is overwritten.

Manages external C/ASM dependencies from Git repositories or the
  official ForgeZero package catalog.

fz pm add github.com/me/my-lib
fz pm add github.com/me/my-lib@v1.2.3
  Clones into vendor/, checks out the version tag if given,
  updates .fz.yaml source_dirs.

fz pm remove my-lib
  Deletes the package directory from vendor/, cleans up
  .fz.yaml, removes empty parent directories.

fz pm list        lists installed packages and versions
fz pm update       pulls latest changes for all installed packages
fz pm catalog      fetches and displays the official catalog
fz pm search iot   searches the catalog by name or keyword
fz pm install esp-idf
  installs a named catalog package with BLAKE3 hash verification

Packages are cloned into vendor/<package-name>/. Network and git
  operations run with configurable timeouts, default 60 seconds,
  overridden by -timeout.

fz sbom
    Generates a CycloneDX SBOM (valid JSON), importable into
    Dependency-Track, Grype, Syft. SBOM artifacts are produced
    atomically, preventing partial output files on crash/interrupt.
    Each component entry: name, version (Git commit SHA for vendored
    packages), BLAKE3 hash, type (source-file, vendored-package,
    system-library), SPDX license identifier.

fz sbom
  fz sbom -o /tmp/myproject-sbom.cdx.json
  fz sbom -dir ./src -o release-sbom.cdx.json

fz audit
    Built-in SAST scanner, three checks:

1. Hardcoded secrets: entropy-weighted regex patterns for AWS
   keys, GitHub tokens, Slack tokens, generic key/secret/
   password/token assignments, PEM private key blocks, plaintext
   connection strings. Findings include file, line, pattern type,
   severity, never the actual secret value.

2. License compliance:
     MPL-2.0                    modified files must release under
                                 MPL                    WARNING
     GPL-2.0 / GPL-3.0          strong copyleft         HIGH
     AGPL-3.0                   network copyleft        HIGH
     proprietary in vendor/     may prohibit             CRITICAL
                                 redistribution

3. Dangerous patterns: gets(), sprintf() without bounds, alloca()
   inside loops, format string vulnerabilities, unchecked
   malloc()/realloc(), chmod 777, curl | sh patterns.

  fz audit
  fz audit -dir ./src
  fz audit -json

Exit code 0 means no findings; 1 means any WARNING or above.

random build IDs               -Wl,--build-id=none
__DATE__/__TIME__ macros       SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH forced to last
                                Git commit timestamp
absolute paths in DWARF info   -fdebug-prefix-map=/abs/path=.
object sort order               lexicographic sort before linking

fz -dir ./src --reproducible

Verification: build the same source/commit on two machines with
  --reproducible and compare sha256sum output; both hashes must match.

The manifest is plain text: one BLAKE3 hex hash plus relative path
  per line.

fz verify
fz verify -manifest ./release.manifest
fz verify --strict     (also reports UNTRACKED files)

Three finding categories: MODIFIED, MISSING, UNTRACKED (--strict
  only). Symlink boundary protection is always active and cannot be
  disabled; every symlink target is resolved and checked against the
  project root, and symlinks escaping the boundary are skipped with a
  warning.

fz bench
fz bench -dir ./src
fz bench -n 5
fz bench -json

Nanosecond-precision timing per build phase (cache check, each
  compile step, pre-link symbol check, link), with percentage of
  total. -n N runs N times and reports average plus standard
  deviation per phase. -json emits total_ns, total_ms, cache state,
  and a phases array with per-phase start_ns/duration_ns.

Generates CONTRIBUTING_USER.md in the current directory, containing:
  environment diagnostics (from fz doctor, reflecting the actual
  machine state), build instructions derived from the active
  .fz.yaml or CLI defaults, standard go test / coverage / lint
  recommendations, guidance for finding "good first issue" tickets,
  and branch-naming/commit-message/PR-description guidelines. Written
  via an atomic write, mode 0644; an existing CONTRIBUTING_USER.md is
  not overwritten without confirmation.

Generates compile_commands.json, read by: Neovim (clangd, via
  Mason, auto-detected), VSCode (clangd extension, point at project
  root), CLion (built-in, auto-opened), Helix (clangd, set in
  languages.toml), Emacs (eglot or lsp-mode, both read the file from
  the project root).

Fetches the latest release binary, backs up the current binary to
  fz.old, installs the new one, reports the version change.

Package internal/fs. Consumers: internal/utils, internal/doctor,
  internal/verify, internal/sbom, internal/pkgman. All durable and
  security-sensitive I/O routes through a FileSystem interface rather
  than direct os.Open / os.WriteFile / os.Rename calls, to enable
  deterministic fault-injection tests and to centralize symlink and
  path-substitution defenses in one audited path.

Interface surface: MkdirAll, WriteFile, ReadFile, Open, OpenVerified,
  CreateTemp, Remove, RemoveAll, Rename, Stat, Lstat, ReadDir, Chmod,
  Readlink, EvalSymlinks, SameFile. fs.Default is bound to Unix{} or
  Windows{} at compile time via build tags.

OpenVerified on Unix, TOCTOU mitigation:
    1. Lstat the path, metadata without following a final symlink.
    2. If the path is itself a symlink, reject outright
       (ErrSymlink); Lstat inspects the link node itself rather than
       following it, which is why Lstat is used instead of Stat.
    3. Open the path, then Stat the opened file descriptor.
    4. Compare identity (os.SameFile) between the pre-open Lstat
       result and the post-open Stat result; a mismatch means the
       path changed between check and use (ErrPathChanged), closing
       the classic check-then-use race window.

Windows implementation is a separate compilation unit selected by
  build tags (unix.go / default_unix.go / rename_unix.go for
  !windows; windows.go / default_windows.go / rename_windows.go for
  windows), not a runtime branch. pathnorm.go (CleanPath,
  HasDrivePrefix, IsUNC, NormalizeAbs) is shared across platforms.
  OpenVerified on Windows follows the same logical steps as Unix.

fs.Mock is a test double that injects per-operation errors
  (ErrDiskFull, ErrPermission, ErrTimeout, ErrInterrupted, ErrSymlink,
  ErrPathChanged) via SetFailOp / SetFail, swapped in at runtime with
  utils.SetFileSystem for tests only.

Consumer integration: SecureWriteFile uses MkdirAll, CreateTemp,
  Chmod, write, close, Rename, Chmod. ReadFileSecure uses
  ResolveSecurePath then OpenVerified then read. CopyFile uses
  OpenVerified on the source, a temp file, then Rename. StatResolved
  and ReadDirResolved use Stat/ReadDir on already-resolved paths.

Packages: internal/utils, internal/fs, internal/pkgman,
  internal/assembler, internal/linker, internal/zig.

Command hardening, the RunCommand pipeline. Every external process
  fz spawns (git, ar, zig, fasm, gcc, g++, clang, ld, nasm, objdump,
  nm, readelf) passes through utils.RunCommand:
    stage 1  argument validation: ValidateCLIArg rejects shell
             metacharacters, backticks, pipes, redirection symbols,
             embedded NUL/newline bytes
    stage 2  absolute executable resolution via exec.LookPath; no
             bare-name invocations
    stage 3  per-argument sanitization, preventing injection via
             malicious config flags or crafted filenames
    stage 4  fixed environment: LC_ALL=C, LANG=C (stable locale
             sorting/diagnostics), TZ=UTC (reproducible timestamps),
             SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=1600000000 (aligns with reproducible
             build expectations)
    stage 5  cmd.Dir set to the project execution root, so relative
             paths in tool invocations resolve inside the project tree

Atomic writes, SecureWriteFile. atomicWrite algorithm: CreateTemp in
  the destination directory (same volume, for atomic rename), Chmod
  the temp file to 0600, write the full payload, Close (flush to
  kernel), rename the temp file over the resolved destination, Chmod
  the destination to 0600. Files written this way: .fz.yaml updates
  from fz pm, .fz.manifest, compile_commands.json, SBOM output,
  CONTRIBUTING_USER.md, and fz -init templates.

Constant-time toolchain checksum verification. .fz.yaml may specify
  expected BLAKE3 digests per tool binary under tool_checksums (e.g.
  gcc, nasm). Comparison uses crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare to
  remove timing side channels; a mismatch fails the build before any
  compilation with a "tool checksum mismatch" error.

Execution root and path confinement: EnsureInsideRoot,
  HashDirWithRoot, and doctor's scanTree use ResolveSecurePath to
  ensure scanned paths do not escape the root via symlinks or ".."
  segments after evaluation; described as an invariant, not a
  configurable option.

Package internal/doctor. A pre-flight diagnostic; it does not
  compile code. It answers whether the current machine satisfies fz's
  minimum operational requirements.

fz doctor
fz doctor -root /path/to/project
fz doctor -json
fz doctor -root ./myapp -json

Four-stage audit pipeline:
    stage A  toolchain reachability: probes zig (required), fasm
             (required on non-Windows), wasm-ld (optional)
    stage B  recursive permission audit: writes .fz_doctor_probe via
             SecureWriteFile, walks the entire tree with
             OpenVerifiedRead on every regular file, counts
             DirsScanned and FilesSeen
    stage C  platform integrity: goos, goarch, filesystem
             implementation name (unix/windows), execution root, CPU
             count
    stage D  health aggregation: healthy is false if any required
             tool is missing, or if readable/writable is false
CI gate usage: fz doctor -json | jq -e ".healthy"

Backend selection is compile-time via GOOS build tags, not a
  runtime if runtime.GOOS branch inside OpenVerified:
    GOOS=linux    compiles unix.go, default_unix.go, rename_unix.go
    GOOS=windows  compiles windows.go, default_windows.go,
                  rename_windows.go
    GOOS=darwin   compiles unix.go with a stubbed syscall layer;
                  builds succeed, runtime behavior is stated as
                  untested

Platform-specific syscall drivers: Linux uses golang.org/x/sys/unix
  with direct SYS_OPENAT/SYS_UNLINKAT; Windows uses
  golang.org/x/sys/windows with CreateFile/ReadFile/WriteFile; Darwin
  uses a stubbed syscall layer (builds succeed, runtime untested).
  openHot and unlinkHot use fixed-size stack buffers for UTF-8 path
  conversion on all platforms, described as achieving zero heap
  allocation in the hot path.

Atomic rename on Windows uses bounded retry (up to 8 attempts,
  10ms/20ms/30ms.../80ms backoff) to tolerate sharing violations from
  antivirus or indexing software holding a handle.

Cross-architecture validation: builds verified for amd64 and arm64
  across Linux, Windows, and Darwin, with no CGO dependencies (pure Go
  plus static C linkage where required); binary digests are described
  as stable across rebuilds with identical inputs on validated
  platforms.

Coverage targets by package: internal/pkgman, internal/fs,
  internal/doctor, internal/config, internal/zig, and internal/gloria
  at 90 percent or higher; internal/man at 100 percent;
  internal/assembler in the high 80s to 90s percent; internal/linker
  in the high 70s to 80s percent.

Zero-allocation enforcement: allocation-tracking benchmarks for
  Linux and Windows targets enforce zero-allocation guarantees in
  critical paths (BenchmarkCopyFileHot, BenchmarkResolveSymbols,
  BenchmarkEmitRelocations all expected at 0 allocs/op, 0 B/op).
  Failure on any allocs/op above 0 in hot-path benchmarks blocks
  merge.

Fault injection via fs.Mock, e.g. forcing CreateTemp to fail with
  ErrDiskFull and asserting SecureWriteFile returns an error with no
  partial final file written.

Race and integration: go test ./... -race; go test
  ./internal/... -coverprofile=coverage.out; go tool cover
  -func=coverage.out.

Static analysis: 100 percent compliance with golangci-lint under
  strict configuration (gofmt, govet, staticcheck, unused enabled),
  zero lint warnings in the main branch, required on all PRs.

Gloria test infrastructure: patchVGA() replaces the compile-time
  0xB8000 constant with a heap-allocated fake VGA buffer for the
  duration of a test, undone via t.Cleanup; dumpVGA() renders that
  buffer to stdout via syscall.Write with zero heap allocations, used
  in golden-file tests to verify expected screen output.

Contributor requirements for PRs touching internal/fs,
  internal/utils I/O or RunCommand, internal/doctor, or
  internal/gloria: table-driven tests for new branches, fs.Mock or
  existing seams for failure paths, no regression below the 90
  percent coverage bar, go test ./... passing locally, golangci-lint
  strict mode passing, and benchmark assertions for new hot-path code
  at 0 allocs/op.
HADES ENGINE, CODEGEN AND ELF EMISSION

The HADES engine is fz's integrated codegen and ELF emission layer,
  transforming parsed AST nodes into deterministic ELF64 object files
  without external assembler binaries for supported instruction sets.
  It is the exclusive backend for Gloria (.glo) source and is also
  used internally for hot-path object emission in the linker.

ELF emitter refactor:
    - symbol table ordering: local symbols are unconditionally
      emitted before global symbols in .symtab, satisfying strict
      linker compliance; earlier versions emitted symbols in parse
      order, which could produce technically-linkable but
      non-compliant objects
    - absolute relocation offsets: call/jmp target resolution is
      recalculated to correctly compute absolute relocation offsets,
      validated via a factorial execution test producing exit code
      120 (factorial of 5)
    - instruction/label disambiguation: the lexer/parser boundary
      strictly separates CPU instruction mnemonics from user-defined
      labels, eliminating prior ambiguity where certain two-character
      mnemonics at column zero could be misidentified as label
      prefixes

Parser integrity guards: malformed input that previously produced
  incomplete or silently incorrect AST nodes now triggers an explicit
  malformed-AST error before codegen begins; silent fallback
  degradation into a broken-but-structurally-valid ELF is described as
  no longer possible.

Codegen zero-allocation path: resolveSymbols, emitRelocations, and
  copyFileHot are refactored to eliminate all heap allocations from
  the hot path, using fixed-capacity stack-allocated intermediate
  buffers; the pipeline is stated to run at hardware memory bandwidth
  limits, about 1.18 GB/s on the Intel i5-10310U test machine, with
  zero GC interference.

Extended instruction emission: emitMovMemToReg64 and
  emitMovRegToMem64 for arbitrary 64-bit memory reads/writes;
  emitPushReg / emitPopReg extended to registers R8 through R15;
  emitBareMetalPrint writes directly to the VGA framebuffer at
  0xB8000; emitLowLevelPrint dispatches to sys_write in userspace or
  the VGA path in kernel mode. parseStringLiteral resolves \n and \t
  escapes at compile time, so no runtime string scanning occurs.

Direct linker bypass, -ld: exposes the HADES linker invocation
  directly, bypassing the gcc/clang wrapper used in -mode auto and
  -mode c. Distinct from -mode raw, which selects ld over gcc at the
  linking stage; -ld removes the compiler driver from the picture
  entirely at the build orchestration level. Overhead reduction is
  about 3 to 5 percent on small projects; on large builds dominated by
  codegen, the difference is described as negligible.

ForgeZero removes the friction between writing assembly (or C, C++, Objective-C, or Gloria) code and running it. Instead of managing assembler flags, linker invocations, and object file paths by hand, you point fz at a source file or directory and it handles everything:

items.content: Detects the file type and selects the correct assembler backend automatically.
Compiles each source file into an object file with appropriate flags.
Checks for duplicate global symbols across all objects before linking.
Links everything into a single binary using the most appropriate linker.
Caches compiled objects using BLAKE3 so unchanged files are never recompiled.
Optionally watches the filesystem and rebuilds on every save.
Emits structured JSON build reports for CI/CD integration.
Generates compile_commands.json for full LSP and IDE integration.
Supports cross-compilation to ARM, RISC-V, WASM, and other targets via -target.
Builds static libraries (.a) and shared libraries (.so / .dylib) in addition to executables.
Compiles C++ (.cpp, .cc, .cxx) with the same strict standards as C.
Compiles Objective-C (.m) via Clang/Zig with automatic framework linking.
Compiles Gloria (.glo) directly to raw x86-64 ELF binaries with zero external dependencies.
Manages external C/ASM dependencies via the built-in package manager (fz pm).
Generates CycloneDX SBOMs with BLAKE3 hashes for supply chain transparency.
Runs a built-in SAST scanner to detect secrets, license violations, and dangerous patterns.
Guarantees byte-identical reproducible builds across machines.
Verifies source tree integrity via BLAKE3 manifests.
Profiles every build phase with nanosecond precision (fz bench).
Compiles to WebAssembly via wasm32-emscripten and wasm32-wasi.
Routes security-sensitive filesystem operations through a virtual layer with TOCTOU-safe OpenVerified reads (v3.1.0 Aegis).
Runs fz doctor to verify toolchain presence, directory permissions, and platform integrity before builds.
Achieves zero heap allocations on all linker hot-paths — 0 allocs/op, 0 B/op in micro-benchmarks (v4.1.0 Citadel).
Uses stack-buffered syscalls (openHot, unlinkHot) for path conversion, bypassing os.File and path/filepath heap overhead.
Emits correct, deterministic ELF64 binaries with local-before-global symbol table ordering and fixed absolute relocation offsets (HADES engine, v4.1.0).
Applies build profiles (-profile / -p) to tune CPU core usage and optimization level per-session, persisted across runs (v4.7.0).
Generates CONTRIBUTING_USER.md via fz contribute with environment diagnostics and contributor guidance (v4.7.0).

Build profiles (-profile / -p) — three named profiles (performance, balanced, power-saver) that set GOMAXPROCS, -j, and the compiler optimization level in a single flag. The active profile persists between runs in ~/.config/fz/.profile.config. Short form -p is equivalent to -profile.

Contributor guidance (fz contribute) — generates CONTRIBUTING_USER.md in the current directory. The file includes environment diagnostics (via fz doctor), test and build recommendations, good-first-issue hints, and PR / commit guidelines.
Version output refinement — fz -v prints the short version string (4.7.0); fz --version prints the full details block including platform, Go version, and build metadata.

Objective-C support — .m files are now a first-class extension. Compilation is automatically delegated to the Clang or Zig backend. System libraries and Objective-C runtime frameworks are linked automatically with standardized diagnostic output. The full pipeline — from utility and parser recognition through intermediate assembly generation — is end-to-end. Verbose build output correctly displays each Objective-C compilation stage.

RISC-V + musl cross-compilation — riscv64-linux-musl is now a validated static-target toolchain. Musl runtime components, linker argument generation, and supported architectures have extended validation and test coverage.
Gloria reliability — improved error propagation in Gloria builtins; safer resource cleanup in runtime tests; explicit handling of walk and mapping errors.
Maintenance — removed unused helpers and dead code; simplified internal implementations; modernized file operations to current Go APIs; improved lint compliance across the codebase.

Technical — added musl linker argument validation tests; added runtime file verification tests; extended RISC-V target coverage; improved linker integration tests; refactored assembler, linker, builder, and plugin subsystems.
Embedded musl runtime — musl runtime for Linux x86_64 is now embedded directly into the fz binary via go:embed. No external musl installation is required for static linking on the host architecture.
Update mechanism reliability — improved self-update stability; linker and build system stability improvements.
while loops — conditional iteration based on a variable value. Supports =, +=, -= assignment and builtin calls inside the loop body.
peek / poke — 16-bit memory read/write at arbitrary addresses. Both accept immediate integers or variable names.
I/O port primitives — in8(port) reads a byte from an x86-64 I/O port (zero-extended); out8(port, value) writes a byte.

VGA framebuffer output — print() in bare-metal mode writes directly to 0xB8000 with green text attribute 0x0A. Register R15 is reserved as the VGA cursor offset and is preserved across function calls. Escape sequences \n and \t are resolved at compile time.
Register constants — named constants for all 16 x86-64 general-purpose registers added for codegen clarity.
Extended register support — emitPushReg and emitPopReg now support R8–R15.
Test infrastructure — patchVGA() replaces the 0xB8000 constant with a heap-allocated fake VGA buffer; dumpVGA() renders the buffer to stdout using syscall.Write (zero allocations).

Zero-allocation linker hot-path — copyFileHot, resolveSymbols, and emitRelocations achieve 0 allocs/op and 0 B/op in all micro-benchmarks. GC pressure on the hot path is eliminated entirely. If you find an allocation in these paths, it is a bug.

Stack-buffered syscalls — openHot and unlinkHot use fixed-size stack buffers for UTF-8 path conversion, removing all transient heap objects from the kernel boundary. Sustained copy throughput reaches ~1.18 GB/s on Intel i5-10310U hardware with zero GC interference.

HADES ELF emitter refactor — local symbols now precede global symbols in .symtab, satisfying strict linker compliance. Absolute relocation offsets for call and jmp targets are calculated deterministically (verified via factorial execution test, exit code 120). Lexer/parser boundary checks prevent CPU instructions from being misidentified as user-defined labels.
Direct linker invocation (-ld) — new flag bypasses compiler validation layers and invokes the linker directly, reducing orchestration overhead by ~3–5% on small projects.
Arena size override (--arena-size=N) — overrides default arena capacity for plugin execution (advanced use).
Extended benchmark coverage — 200-module and 400-module data points added; 4.95× speedup confirmed at 400 modules.

Platform-specific syscall drivers — conditional compilation via //go:build linux, windows, darwin build tags. Linux uses direct SYS_OPENAT / SYS_UNLINKAT via golang.org/x/sys/unix; Windows uses native CreateFile / ReadFile / WriteFile via golang.org/x/sys/windows; Darwin is stubbed with an isolated syscall layer.
Cross-architecture validation — verified builds for amd64 and arm64 across all target OSes; no CGO dependencies, pure Go + static C linkage.
100% golangci-lint compliance — strict configuration (-E gofmt,govet,staticcheck,unused), zero lint warnings in main branch.
Backward compatible — v4.1.0 is fully compatible with v4.0.x configurations and CLI flags. No migration required.

Virtual filesystem abstraction (internal/fs) — all durable I/O routes through a FileSystem interface with Unix and native Windows implementations. OpenVerified closes the TOCTOU window between metadata check and read open via Lstat + SameFile identity verification.

Hardened subprocess execution — external tools (git, ar, zig, fasm, gcc, ld, nasm, …) invoke exclusively through utils.RunCommand, which resolves binaries with exec.LookPath, validates every argument, and runs under a fixed, reproducibility-oriented environment.

Atomic secure writes — manifests, configuration files, SBOM output, and doctor probe files use SecureWriteFile: mode 0600 temporary file, flush via close, platform-specific atomic rename (retry loop on Windows file locks).
Constant-time toolchain checksum comparison — optional per-tool BLAKE3 expectations in config are verified with crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare to reduce timing leakage during integrity audits.

fz doctor — four-stage self-audit: toolchain reachability, recursive permission probe (read/write via OpenVerified), platform integrity (GOOS/GOARCH, VFS backend name, execution root, CPU count), and consolidated human or --json reporting.
Native Windows I/O path — //go:build windows selects fs.Windows, CleanPath for drive/UNC normalization, and renameAtomic with bounded retries when AV software holds transient locks.
Fault-injection test suite — fs.Mock injects ErrDiskFull, ErrPermission, ErrTimeout, and related errors; critical internal packages target 90%+ statement coverage.
Zig toolchain backend — zig cc / zig c++ as primary or alternative backend for C/C++. Zero external dependencies for cross-compilation: Zig ships all headers, libc, and sysroots internally.
SBOM (CycloneDX + BLAKE3) — fz sbom generates a Software Bill of Materials in CycloneDX format with BLAKE3 hashes for every component.
SAST audit (fz audit) — built-in security scanner: hardcoded secrets, license compliance (MPL/GPL detection), and dangerous C patterns.
Reproducible builds (--reproducible) — suppression of build IDs, timestamps, and non-deterministic path references; object files sorted before linking for byte-identical output.
Source tree verification (fz verify) — generates and checks BLAKE3 manifests of the entire source tree.
Symlink boundary protection — every symlink is resolved and validated against the project root, blocking symlink race attacks.
fz bench — nanosecond-precision build profiler. Multi-run averaging and JSON output supported.
Race-free parallel pipeline — verified with go test -race across the complete test suite.
FASM improvements — automatic format ELF64 injection and correct -dDEBUG=1 / debug symbol pass-through.
WebAssembly — wasm32-emscripten and wasm32-wasi targets.
BLAKE3 hashing — cache is up to 7× faster. 10 MB file hash time dropped from ~58 ms to ~8.7 ms.
Package manager (fz pm) — manage external C/ASM dependencies from Git or the official catalog.
Shared library support — -shared, -cc-flag, and -ld-flag flags for .so, .dylib, and .dll targets.
High test coverage — utils 84%, linker 60%, assembler 60%, builder 56%.
Cross-compilation — -target supports ARM, RISC-V, x86_64, and any standard GNU cross-compilation triple.
LSP support — -compile-commands generates compile_commands.json for clangd, ccls, and LSP-aware editors.
Smart self-update — fz -update backs up the old binary to /usr/local/bin/fz.old before replacing it.
Static libraries — -type static and -lib build .a archives via ar.
Unique object file names — multi-directory projects no longer produce colliding .o names.
Parallel builds — -j N compiles all source files concurrently (0 = auto).
Interactive shell — fz -shell opens a REPL for running fz commands without re-invoking the binary.
C++ support — .cpp, .cc, .cxx compiled with g++ or clang++.
Project initialization — fz -init scaffolds .fz.yaml, .fzignore, and README.md.
Flat binary output — -format bin for bootloaders, firmware, and embedded targets.
Multiple source directories — source_dirs scanned in parallel.
Explicit source file lists — source_files bypasses directory scanning entirely.
.fzignore file — .gitignore-style exclusion rules for recursive scanning.
Multi-level config merging — system, user, and project YAML configs merged in priority order.

blocks.content: ForgeZero is intentionally lightweight — a single statically compiled Go binary with no runtime dependencies beyond the standard assembler/compiler toolchain.

items.content: Config subsystem overhaul — internal/config rewritten for deterministic, low-allocation merges: LoadMerged now respects FZ_CONFIG_PATH/FZ_CONFIG environment overrides, merges system→user→local config layers deterministically, and merges list/map fields while preserving user intent. Config.Merge was hardened to merge slices (deduped, ordered), maps (non-empty overrides), and ISO/custom-args properly.
Concurrent config load — multiple config files discovered by FindConfigs() are loaded concurrently and merged deterministically to reduce I/O latency on large projects.

Inline Bash scripts — configuration scripts entries may begin with bash: to include inline Bash code. fz prefers the system bash/sh when available; if not, a small internal runner executes common commands like cd and export and will fall back to executing binaries directly when available.
NASM fallback — when a system nasm binary is not present, fz now falls back to the built-in NASM-like emitter/parser so .asm sources still assemble without an external dependency.
Improved validation & defaults — Validate() and fillDefaults() now ensure safer defaults and clearer error messages for invalid modes, profiles, and isolation values.
Expanded tests — new and updated unit tests for internal/config covering merge semantics, ISO handling, and invalid YAML handling.
Docs & examples — README extended with configuration and testing quickstart guidance (you are reading it).
Precedence: explicit path (CLI or FZ_CONFIG_PATH/FZ_CONFIG) → system config → user config → local project config. The effective configuration is the result of merging these layers in that order.

Merge behavior: string scalars are overridden by later layers when non-empty; slices are concatenated with deduplication preserving earlier order; maps are merged with non-empty values overriding earlier entries; source_file clears directory lists and vice-versa to avoid ambiguous scan behavior.

The -ld flag invokes the linker directly, bypassing compiler validation layers entirely. This reduces orchestration overhead by ~3–5% on small projects. Unlike -mode raw, -ld does not imply a particular linking strategy — it simply removes the gcc/clang wrapper from the invocation chain. Use this when you need the tightest possible control over linker arguments and accept full responsibility for the link step.

Initial release: v3.0.0 GLORIA · JIT features: v4.4.0 · Reliability improvements: v4.5.1

Gloria is ForgeZero's integrated systems programming language. It compiles directly to raw x86-64 ELF binaries via the HADES engine with no external assembler, compiler, or linker required. Gloria is designed for bare-metal targets, OS kernels, firmware, and other environments where binary size and toolchain footprint matter.

items.content: Go/Rust-like syntax with a minimal feature set.
Compiled output: 69–125 bytes for typical programs.
No runtime dependencies — the output is a self-contained ELF64 binary.
Bare-metal VGA framebuffer output via print() when compiled in kernel mode.
x86-64 I/O port access via in8() / out8().
Arbitrary memory read/write via peek() / poke().

blocks.content: Evaluates a variable. If the variable is non-zero, the body executes.
Iterates while the condition variable is non-zero. Supports =, +=, and -= assignment and builtin calls inside the loop body.
peek(address) — reads a 16-bit value from the specified memory address. The address may be an immediate integer literal or a variable.
poke(address, value) — writes a 16-bit value to the specified memory address. Both arguments may be immediates or variables.

poke(0xB8000, 0x0741)   // write 'A' with white-on-black to VGA
let pos = 2
poke(pos, 0x0742)
in8(port) — reads one byte from an x86-64 I/O port. The return value is zero-extended to 64 bits.
out8(port, value) — writes one byte to an x86-64 I/O port.
When Gloria is compiled in bare-metal (kernel) mode, print() writes directly to the VGA text-mode framebuffer at 0xB8000 using green text (attribute byte 0x0A).

items.content: Register R15 is reserved as the VGA cursor offset and is preserved across all function calls.
Escape sequences \n and \t in string literals are parsed and converted to the corresponding control characters at compile time.
emitLowLevelPrint dispatches to the VGA path when kernelMode is set, or to the sys_write syscall path in userspace mode.

blocks.content: In userspace mode (default), print() uses Linux sys_write (fd 1). The kernel/userspace dispatch is automatic based on the compilation target.
Named constants for all 16 x86-64 general-purpose registers are available to Gloria programs and are used internally by the HADES codegen layer for clarity and correctness:
R15 is reserved by the Gloria runtime as the VGA cursor register in bare-metal mode and must not be used as a general-purpose variable in kernel Gloria programs.

fz -gloria main.glo
./main

fz -gloria main.glo -verbose
fz -gloria main.glo -out kernel.elf

items.content: Lexer — tokenizes .glo source; CPU instruction mnemonics are disambiguated from user labels at the lexer/parser boundary (v4.1.0 HADES).
Parser — builds an AST; malformed input triggers ErrMalformedAST before codegen begins; no silent fallback degradation.
Codegen (HADES) — emits x86-64 machine code from the AST using zero-allocation stack buffers.
ELF emission — writes a valid ELF64 binary with correct local-before-global .symtab ordering and deterministic relocation offsets.

blocks.content: The entire pipeline runs in a single process with zero intermediate files on disk. Output binary size is typically 69–125 bytes.

; hello.asm — print "Hello, World!" to stdout and exit
section .data
    msg  db "Hello, World!", 0x0a
    len  equ $ - msg

section .text
    global _start

_start:
    mov rax, 1
    mov rdi, 1
    mov rsi, msg
    mov rdx, len
    syscall

    mov rax, 60
    xor rdi, rdi
    syscall

# hello.s — AT&T syntax
.section .data
    msg: .ascii "Hello, World!\n"
    len = . - msg

.section .text
    .global _start

_start:
    movq  $1,   %rax
    movq  $1,   %rdi
    movq  $msg, %rsi
    movq  $len, %rdx
    syscall

    movq  $60,  %rax
    xorq  %rdi, %rdi
    syscall

blocks.content: ForgeZero v3.1.0 introduces a deliberate separation between what filesystem operations the build tool requires and how the host operating system performs them. Goals: enable deterministic fault-injection tests without patching os globally, and centralize symlink and path-substitution defenses in one audited code path.

type FileSystem interface {
    MkdirAll(path string, perm os.FileMode) error
    WriteFile(path string, data []byte, perm os.FileMode) error
    ReadFile(path string) ([]byte, error)
    Open(path string) (io.ReadCloser, error)
    OpenVerified(path string) (io.ReadCloser, error)
    CreateTemp(dir, pattern string) (*os.File, error)
    Remove(name string) error
    RemoveAll(path string) error
    Rename(oldpath, newpath string) error
    Stat(name string) (os.FileInfo, error)
    Lstat(name string) (os.FileInfo, error)
    ReadDir(name string) ([]os.DirEntry, error)
    Chmod(name string, mode os.FileMode) error
    Readlink(name string) (string, error)
    EvalSymlinks(path string) (string, error)
    SameFile(a, b os.FileInfo) bool
}
Binding model: fs.Default is set to Unix{} or Windows{} at compile time via build tags. utils.fileSystem() returns the active implementation.
Why Lstat? Stat follows symlinks. An attacker could place a symlink at path; Lstat inspects the link node itself and rejects it immediately.
Why SameFile after open? This closes the TOCTOU window:

blocks.content: Every external process spawned by ForgeZero — git, ar, zig, fasm, gcc, g++, clang, ld, nasm, objdump, nm, readelf — must pass through utils.RunCommand.
Stage 1 — Argument validation: ValidateCLIArg rejects shell metacharacters, backticks, pipes, redirection symbols, and embedded NUL/newline bytes.
Stage 2 — Absolute executable resolution: exec.LookPath resolves the binary; no bare-name invocations.
Stage 3 — Argument sanitization: Each argument is validated, preventing injection via malicious config flags or crafted filenames.
Stage 5 — Execution root: cmd.Dir is set to the project execution root so relative paths in tool invocations resolve inside the project tree.

blocks.content: New in v4.1.0 Citadel · Gloria extended in v4.4.0

The HADES engine is ForgeZero's integrated codegen and ELF emission layer. It is responsible for transforming parsed AST nodes into correct, deterministic ELF64 object files without relying on external assembler binaries for supported instruction sets. HADES is the exclusive compilation backend for Gloria (.glo) source files and is also used internally for hot-path object emission in the linker.

Symbol table ordering. The ELF specification requires local symbols to precede global symbols in .symtab. Earlier versions of ForgeZero emitted symbols in parse order, which could produce object files that technically linked but were non-compliant with strict linker expectations. v4.1.0 enforces local-before-global ordering unconditionally.

Absolute relocation offsets. call and jmp target resolution was recalculated to correctly compute offsets for absolute relocations. This was validated via a factorial execution test: a self-referential recursive function compiled and linked through the HADES pipeline produces exit code 120 (factorial(5)), confirming that call targets resolve to the correct runtime address.

Instruction/label disambiguation. The lexer/parser boundary now maintains strict differentiation between CPU instruction mnemonics and user-defined labels. In earlier versions, certain two-character mnemonics could be misidentified as label prefixes when appearing at column zero in specific syntactic contexts. v4.1.0 eliminates this ambiguity with explicit boundary checks, preventing silent object file corruption.

The parser maintains invariants at each AST node boundary. Malformed input that previously produced incomplete or silently incorrect AST nodes now triggers an explicit ErrMalformedAST error before codegen begins. Silent fallback degradation — where a corrupted AST would produce a broken but structurally valid ELF — is no longer possible.

The HADES codegen path (resolveSymbols, emitRelocations, copyFileHot) was refactored to eliminate all heap allocations from the hot path. All intermediate buffers are stack-allocated with fixed capacity. The codegen pipeline operates at hardware memory bandwidth limits (~1.18 GB/s on Intel i5-10310U) with zero GC interference.
If you find an allocation in our hot paths — it's a bug.
The following codegen primitives were added or extended for Gloria JIT support:

rows: Emit MOV reg, [mem] for arbitrary 64-bit memory reads
Emit MOV [mem], reg for arbitrary 64-bit memory writes
Push register to stack; extended to R8–R15
Pop register from stack; extended to R8–R15
Write string directly to VGA framebuffer at 0xB8000
Dispatch to sys_write (userspace) or VGA path (kernel mode)

blocks.content: All new emission functions maintain the zero-allocation invariant. parseStringLiteral resolves \n and \t escape sequences at compile time, so no runtime string scanning occurs.

The -ld flag exposes the HADES linker invocation directly, bypassing the gcc/clang wrapper layer used in -mode auto and -mode c. This is distinct from -mode raw (which selects ld over gcc at the linking stage); -ld removes the compiler driver from the picture entirely at the build orchestration level. Overhead reduction is ~3–5% on small projects; on large builds dominated by codegen, the difference is negligible.
Use -ld when you need precise control over the linker command vector and do not want ForgeZero's compiler validation checks in the critical path.

Benchmarks measured against standard nasm -f elf64 && ld and make -j4 pipelines. Test environment: Intel Core i5-10310U (4C/8T, 1.7 GHz base), Arch Linux, Samsung 980 NVMe. Results are mean ± stddev over ≥10 runs via hyperfine.

Conclusion: ForgeZero maintains ~3–5× speedup at scale, growing to nearly 5× at 400 modules. Traditional pipelines suffer from super-linear overhead due to process spawning, I/O contention, and CPU cache thrashing. ForgeZero's single-process design preserves cache locality across the entire build.

searchIndex.title: CLI-REFERENCE
searchIndex.text: Named presets configuring CPU core usage, parallel job count, and compiler optimization level in a single flag. Persisted between runs in ~/.config/fz/.profile.config and applied automatically on subs

performance    all cores    -O3    all cores    max build speed,

balanced       half core

searchIndex.title: CLI-REFERENCE

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: fz wraps NASM, GAS, FASM, GCC, Clang, Zig, and LD into a single command-line interface. Given a source file or directory, fz:

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE

searchIndex.text: Per source, the traditional pipeline (nasm/gcc + ld, driven by make) spawns one process per compiled unit plus the linker, writes an intermediate object file to disk per unit, and starts each fork wit

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: fz is a single statically compiled Go binary with no runtime dependencies beyond the assembler/compiler toolchain actually invoked for a given source type.

searchIndex.title: INTERNAL MECHANISMS, OVERVIEW
searchIndex.text: ARCHITECTURE — INTERNAL MECHANISMS, OVERVIEW

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Build cache, BLAKE3 Compiled objects are cached in .fz_cache/. Cache key is computed from the BLAKE3 hash of source contents plus the -debug flag state, -mode value, -target value, and active build pr

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Pre-link symbol check Before invoking the linker, fz scans all compiled objects for duplicate global symbol definitions, using nm first, then objdump, then readelf as fallbacks. A conflict is reported

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Watch mode fz -dir ./src -watch uses fsnotify for cross-platform filesystem events. Rebuilds are debounced with a 500 millisecond delay.

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: JSON output -json suppresses normal output and writes one JSON object to stdout on completion: status, exit_code, duration_ms, binary, source_files, object_files, error.

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Clean fz -dir . -clean removes .fz_objs/, .fz_cache/, all .o files, and executable files identified by the +x permission bit.

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Parallel builds -j N compiles up to N files simultaneously; -j 0 uses all available CPU cores. When a profile is active, -j is set automatically by the profile; an explicit -j always overrides it. The

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Interactive shell fz -shell opens a REPL with commands: build , build -dir , set , clean, help, exit.

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: Virtual filesystem layer All durable and security-sensitive I/O routes through an internal/fs FileSystem interface rather than calling os.Open, os.WriteFile, or os.Rename directly. Full detail is in I

searchIndex.title: ARCHITECTURE
searchIndex.text: See LANGUAGES for per-source-type compilation behavior, WORKFLOW for configuration and supply-chain tooling, and INTERNALS for the virtual filesystem layer, the Aegis security core, and the HADES code

searchIndex.title: INTERNALS
searchIndex.text: The HADES engine is fz's integrated codegen and ELF emission layer, transforming parsed AST nodes into deterministic ELF64 object files without external assembler binaries for supported instruction se

searchIndex.title: INTERNALS
searchIndex.text: ELF emitter refactor: - symbol table ordering: local symbols are unconditionally emitted before global symbols in .symtab, satisfying strict linker compliance; earlier versions emitted symbols in pars

searchIndex.title: INTERNALS
searchIndex.text: Parser integrity guards: malformed input that previously produced incomplete or silently incorrect AST nodes now triggers an explicit malformed-AST error before codegen begins; silent fallback degrada

searchIndex.title: INTERNALS

searchIndex.title: INTERNALS

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: Gloria is ForgeZero's integrated systems programming language. It compiles directly to raw x86-64 ELF binaries via the HADES engine with no external assembler, compiler, or linker required. Gloria is

13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: Supported constructs:

searchIndex.title: 13.3 Control Flow
searchIndex.text: 13. Gloria Language — 13.3 Control Flow

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: Evaluates a variable. If the variable is non-zero, the body executes.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: Iterates while the condition variable is non-zero. Supports = , += , and -= assignment and builtin calls inside the loop body.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
13.4 Memory Access & I/O Ports
searchIndex.text: 13. Gloria Language — 13.4 Memory Access & I/O Ports

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: peek(address) — reads a 16-bit value from the specified memory address. The address may be an immediate integer literal or a variable.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: poke(address, value) — writes a 16-bit value to the specified memory address. Both arguments may be immediates or variables.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text:
poke(0xB8000, 0x0741)   // write 'A' with white-on-black to VGA
let pos = 2
poke(pos, 0x0742)

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: in8(port) — reads one byte from an x86-64 I/O port. The return value is zero-extended to 64 bits.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: let scancode = in8(0x60)

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: out8(port, value) — writes one byte to an x86-64 I/O port.

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: out8(0x3F8, 65)   // transmit 'A' on COM1

searchIndex.title: 13.5 VGA Framebuffer Output
searchIndex.text: 13. Gloria Language — 13.5 VGA Framebuffer Output

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: When Gloria is compiled in bare-metal (kernel) mode, print() writes directly to the VGA text-mode framebuffer at 0xB8000 using green text ( attribute byte 0x0A ).

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: In userspace mode (default), print() uses Linux sys_write (fd 1). The kernel/userspace dispatch is automatic based on the compilation target.

searchIndex.title: 13.6 Register Constants
searchIndex.text: 13. Gloria Language — 13.6 Register Constants

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: Named constants for all 16 x86-64 general-purpose registers are available to Gloria programs and are used internally by the HADES codegen layer for clarity and correctness:

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: R15 is reserved by the Gloria runtime as the VGA cursor register in bare-metal mode and must not be used as a general-purpose variable in kernel Gloria programs.

searchIndex.title: 13.7 Compilation Pipeline
searchIndex.text: 13. Gloria Language — 13.7 Compilation Pipeline

fz -gloria main.glo

fz -gloria main.glo -verbose
fz -gloria main.glo -out kernel.elf

searchIndex.title: 13. Gloria Language
searchIndex.text: The entire pipeline runs in a single process with zero intermediate files on disk. Output binary size is typically 69–125 bytes.

searchIndex.text: 36. Aegis Security Core

searchIndex.title: 36.1 Command Hardening: The `RunCommand` Pipeline
searchIndex.text: 36. Aegis Security Core — 36.1 Command Hardening: The `RunCommand` Pipeline

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Every external process spawned by ForgeZero — git , ar , zig , fasm , gcc , g++ , clang , ld , nasm , objdump , nm , readelf — must pass through utils.RunCommand .

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Stage 1 — Argument validation: ValidateCLIArg rejects shell metacharacters, backticks, pipes, redirection symbols, and embedded NUL/newline bytes.

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Stage 2 — Absolute executable resolution: exec.LookPath resolves the binary; no bare-name invocations.

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Stage 3 — Argument sanitization: Each argument is validated, preventing injection via malicious config flags or crafted filenames.

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Stage 4 — Fixed environment:

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Stage 5 — Execution root: cmd.Dir is set to the project execution root so relative paths in tool invocations resolve inside the project tree.

tool_checksums:

searchIndex.title: 36. Aegis Security Core
searchIndex.text: Comparison uses crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare to remove timing side-channels. Mismatch produces tool checksum mismatch for and fails the build before any compilation.

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text: fz doctor is a pre-flight diagnostic. It does not compile code. It answers whether the current machine satisfies ForgeZero's minimum operational requirements.

searchIndex.title: 37.1 Invocation
searchIndex.text: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`) — 37.1 Invocation

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text:
fz doctor
fz doctor -root /path/to/project
fz doctor -json
fz doctor -root ./myapp -json

searchIndex.title: 37.2 Audit Pipeline (Four Stages)
searchIndex.text: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`) — 37.2 Audit Pipeline (Four Stages)

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text: Stage A — Toolchain Reachability: probes zig (required), fasm (required on non-Windows), wasm-ld (optional).

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text: Stage B — Recursive Permission Audit: writes .fz_doctor_probe via SecureWriteFile , then walks the entire tree with OpenVerifiedRead on every regular file. Counts DirsScanned and FilesSeen .

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text: Stage C — Platform Integrity:

searchIndex.title: 37. System Self-Audit (`fz doctor`)
searchIndex.text: Stage D — Health Aggregation: Healthy = false if any required tool is missing, or if Readable or Writable is false.
